subject: RPG / Solo Journaling RPGs / Thousand Year Old Campfire
text:
Technical Series No. 9

Old Morris Cave:

A Continuous Use Campsite in

Mammoth Cave National Park

an excavation by

Prof. Timothy Hutchings et al.

An archeaological excavation conducted

for the St. Louis Museum of Ethnology,

with support from Brandle University,

the Cadley Melton Jr. Foundation, and

the Kentucky Antiquities Commission.

Completed under contract no. 14-10-3:455-2-1978

with the National Park Service.

Published 1980 by

The St. Louis Museum of Ethnology

and

Brandle University

as the 9th release in this collaborative technical series

subject: RPG / Solo Journaling RPGs / Thousand Year Old Campfire
text: 2 3 Old Morris Cave is a living limestone cave system on the northern edge of Mammoth Cave National Park in Kentucky. The region features a complex karst topography and is situated on the eastern slope of the Woolsey Valley, forming the edge of the Chester Upland. The limestone in the cave is primarly Girkin and St. Louis formation with deposits of chert and gypsum, the latter having been mostly removed by early native peoples. The cave is two meters above the Old Prong Creek’s average water level. The creek was dammed at least twice in the 19th Century, bringing the waterline to within half a meter of the cave entrance. Though Old Morris is an outflow cave, the entrance is dry; temperature and fluorescein tracing in the 1950s indicated that the cave’s outflow passes through an anastomic network terminating in a cold seep that drains directly into Old Prong Creek approximately 75 meters (250 feet) downstream of the main entrance. Since at least 2000 B.C., caves in the area have been utilized for shelter and mineral resources by Native Americans. Old Morris Cave in particular has been in almost continuous use as a campsite since at least 500 B.C., playing host to a shifting population over the centuries. Old Morris Cave was first recorded in 1798 when it was described in a land purchase by Valentine Simons, a mining speculator from Baltimore, Maryland. Though Mammoth Cave and other local features were mined for saltpeter and gypsum, Old Morris Cave lacked these resources in any quantity and so was left unexploited. Neither was it commercialized during the caving fad of the early 20th century—Old Morris was neither deep nor beautiful nor convenient to any major travel artery. It was occasionally visited by locals but never developed as a show cave. Due to infrequent flood action from Dry Prong Creek, the floor pack in Old Morris Cave is up to 1.5 meters (5 feet) deep in places, making the cave an excellent site for an archaeological excavation. Additionally, the infrequent inflow of colluvium makes for clear stratigraphic delineation. Three previous professional excavations were staged at the cave in 1932, 1947, and 1962. All three digs (and numerous private treasure hunts and excavations) focused on the deepest recesses of the cave, seeking both hidden artifacts and new cave sections. The 1979 Hutchings dig described in this report focused on the sediment infill mound immediately inside the cave mouth. It is a convenient and well-ventilated place for contemporary visitors to build a campfire and the excavation leader saw no reason that the logic would not hold true for visitors in the cave’s past. The Hutchings dig focused on six excavation columns centered on the most likely campfire build site. Soil from the columns was extracted, screened and, at project’s end, used as backfill. Historical finds were recorded in situ and then sent to the St. Louis Museum of Ethnography for further study. Excavators recorded numerous cultural finds indicating that cave occupation dates to

subject: RPG / Solo Journaling RPGs / Thousand Year Old Campfire
text: the most likely campfire build site. Soil from the columns was extracted, screened and, at project’s end, used as backfill. Historical finds were recorded in situ and then sent to the St. Louis Museum of Ethnography for further study. Excavators recorded numerous cultural finds indicating that cave occupation dates to at least 500 B.C., including multiple campfires, artifacts and food remains, and living floors preserved under alluvial flood deposits and rock fall, various artifacts and food remains. Moderate disturbance from erosion, animal burrows, and recent vandalism was observed and will be accounted for in the interpretation of the site. Special thanks are extended to student volunteers from the Kentucky University Soil Studies program who collected sediment samples that will later be used to cross-date the finds collected and for new research into the history of soil formation in the Chester Upland. Further thanks are extended to the Park City Rotary, the Barren County Sheriff’s Office, Roland Brenton Thomas of the National Park Service, and Art Claverie and the Board of the St. Louis Museum of Ethnography, without whose cooperation none of this could have been possible. INTRODUCTION TO THE PROJECT

subject: RPG / Solo Journaling RPGs / Thousand Year Old Campfire
text: 4 5 The processual archaeological excavation of Old Morris Cave is a stratigraphic excavation in which project members carefully remove layer after layer of soil and record what is exposed. A history of the formation of the cave floor is revealed by the distinct layers. Artifacts exposed can be dated using the chemistry and arrangement of the associated soil. This project is not interested in the soil layering except as a tool for establishing a chronology of the cave. The deeper we dig the older the finds uncovered, but by tracking differentiated layers of soil we will be able to more accurately date and contextualize the historical objects uncovered. For the purposes of this report we shall build the layers of strata in the order of its original formation, from the bottom up, and populate that stratigraphy with finds* where appropriate. You will need a six-sided die, a pencil, an eraser, a blue ballpoint pen, and a print-out of pages 6 and 7 (or you can work in the book). We build the cave strata and discover the location of finds using the following procedure: 1. Find the Strata Chart and Upper Cave Wall section on the next page spread. These record sheets are the focus of our markmaking for the duration of this project; they may be photocopied as needed. Additional instances appear in the back of this report. 2. At the bottom of the Strata Chart the word BEGIN appears at the 60” mark. This is the limestone bottom of the floor section we excavated; we could dig no farther. The top of the chart is contemporary ground level in 1979. 3. Roll a six-sided die and progress upwards that many one- inch increments on the chart. That is how many inches of soil get added this round. From that new number, draw a slightly wavy horizontal line across the chart; this is the new topmost layer of soil. Let the line rise or fall as it passes over earlier finds or disturbances in the previous layer. Use pencil, because this work will require amendation. 3a. If a number is rolled that would extend the stratum beyond the top of the Strata Chart, cease play. Sign the bottom of the chart “Tim Hutchings, Excavation Leader” in blue ballpoint pen, then submit it for peer review. 4. To the right of the current soil surface number the instruction “See Fig x” may appear. You will find that referenced Figure farther along in this report. 5. Each Figure page is a flow chart. Begin at START and end after reaching a square; if a dotted line progresses farther, then push on. At forks in the path use a six-sided die to determine which path is taken. Some of the flow-chart cells will be marked with a dagger, a campfire icon, or other symbol. Instructions for these appear at the bottom of the page; most will direct you to a “Find Entry” number at the end of this report. Address the

subject: RPG / Solo Journaling RPGs / Thousand Year Old Campfire
text: to determine which path is taken. Some of the flow-chart cells will be marked with a dagger, a campfire icon, or other symbol. Instructions for these appear at the bottom of the page; most will direct you to a “Find Entry” number at the end of this report. Address the changes to the cave after finishing with the flow chart. Unless instructed otherwise, draw finds on top of the most recently drawn layer line. The scale of the finds need not be precise; the drawings of the finds themselves need not be perfectly realized. 6. Fill in the new stratigraphic layer with a pattern to differentiate it from the layer below. 7. Return to step one and repeat this process until the end condition in 3a is met. AN EXPLANATION OF METHODOLOGY An example of strata building using the procedure described above. In this image are seven layers of strata containing animal remains and recent trash, while in the upper right we see a burrow filled with seeds. *A “find” in the context of this excavation means any object or disturbance, natural or man-made, which is not a product of the processes that created or laid down the soil in that place.

Methodology Summary

1. Roll a d6 and Count up that many inches from the current

surface line. STart at “begin” on the first Roll.

2. draw a roughly horizontal line to indicate the new top layer.

allow the line to gently undulate and react to previous finds.

3. consult ANY FIGURE NUMBER indicated, and then navigate the

associated flow chart using a d6.

4. AS DIRECTED BY THE FLOW CHART, MAKE CHANGES TO AND ADD OBJECTS

ATOP THE NEW STRATUM, AND ADD OR CHANGE MARKS ON THE “UPPER

CAVE WALL.”

5. Fill the new stratum in with a unique pattern to differentiate it

from other layers. Samples appear on the left side of the chart.

6. Return to line one and repeat.

Figure 37

Find Entry 1 Animal Remains

Animal or insect remains are found in the cave. These may be

a single bone or beetle shell, or more complete remnants.

Skeletons seldom remain articulated. Remains lie flat on the

floor and can appear in any column.

Find Entry 2 Shifting Stone

Rock from the cave ceiling or walls has fallen onto the floor.

Place d6+1 pieces of rock in the cave. These rock pieces are

roughly d6” by d6”; roll separately for the size of each. They fall

in randomly determined columns and can stack up one on top

of another. If rocks fall onto non-rock items, those items will

break or move as appropriate.

Fallen rocks are solid black. If rocks are ever exposed by a

firepit digging action they are moved up to the edge of the

newly dug hole.

The fallen rock may be limestone, shale, or even gypsum.

The Nature of the Find, Its Placement,

and Advice Thereon

The single most important rule in the placement of finds is that precision

in following the directions is not required. Place items according to the

suggestions of the flow chart narrative or according to the Find Entry

instructions, below.

¤

¤

¤

¤

subject: RPG / Solo Journaling RPGs / Thousand Year Old Campfire
text:
48 49

Find Entry 3 Insect Burrows

Burrowing insects have dug down into the cave floor. Add

d6+1 burrows that angle down forty-five degrees into the cave

floor. They are d6+2” deep and will curve around rocks or other

items, deforming their paths when crossing strata.

Find Entry 4 Animal Food Caches

One or more animals have lived here in burrows or buried food

against future need.

First determine the type of hole. A 1-3 indicates it is a simple food

cache, and a 4-6 indicates a burrow system.

Food caches will have d6 holes. Randomly choose a column and

draw a narrow hole d6” deep. Add seeds or nuts to the bottom

of the hole.

On a 4-6 the this is a burrow system with 1-3 living chambers.

Randomly choose a column for entry and a different column for

each living chamber. Each chamber will be 3d6” deep, measuring

from the surface to the top of the chamber ceiling. Connect the

entrance to the chambers with a tunnel.

There is a one in six chance that a burrow system contains one or

more animal skeletons.

Find Entry 5 Hidden Objects, Buried

Some person has buried an object, precious or unwanted.

The object lies in a hole. If a hole or depression more than six inches

deep already exists then that declivity will be the starting place of a

new hole, and can expand in width or depth if necessary. Otherwise

the object lies in a new hole deep enough to put the top of the object

d6” below ground, or 3d6” if the object is organic or otherwise

subject to rot or other biological processes.

If the hole reveals an item, the item will be removed from the cave.

Unless specified otherwise, the hole will then be filled in and leveled

off. Spoil from the hole will form a layer over and around the top.

On a 1-3 the hole will also be covered with a rock or piece of log.

If no such material is present in the cave then new material must

enter in from outside.

Find Entry 6 Valuable Trash

Detritus is present from humans living and working in the cave.

This may mean beads, hooks, needles, arrowheads, cane torches,

bits of bone, hard vegetable debris, chips from flintknapping, broken

or worn out tools, or pieces of pottery or basketwork.

There can be any number of these objects; the majority lie in

columns one and six.

Find Entry 7 Lost Objects

A period-appropriate personal possession was lost in the cave. This

could be a pipe, jewelry or ritual object, a pouch or wallet, eyeglasses

or a flashlight, a knife or a piece of clothing or a book. The item will

be missed by its owner.

A lost item may appear in any column.

subject: RPG / Solo Journaling RPGs / Thousand Year Old Campfire
text:
50 51

Find Entry 8 A Campfire

A firepit or campfire site has been prepared in the cave.

Therefore a fire site must be positioned, dug out, possibly lined,

and then filled with ash.

A fire site will cover columns three and four, filling about a third

of the Strata Chart’s width.

Unless the original entry says otherwise, a firepit site will be dug

out to 2d6-4 inches deep (with a minimum depth of 0”), measuring

from the average level of the cave floor (not from the top of an ash

pile or bottom of a preexisting pit). The hole does not need to be

tidy or symmetrical.

If an item is uncovered during the excavation, roll a die and

consult the following chart to determine what happens. (Note:

this does not appy to stones. See below.)

1. The item disappears from the cave.

2-3. The item is moved onto the cave floor in

column one or six.

4-5. The item is broken and moved onto the cave

floor in column one or six.

6. The item is left where it is. Organic material

will be destroyed by fire; pottery and other

man-made materials become potsherds

or are otherwise broken.

A fire pit may be lined with stones. If stones are visible anywhere

in the cave, including in the recently dug firepit, place them at the

edge of the pit. Partially buried stones will leave holes when they

are moved.

Finally, a campfire site will fill with ash. Roll d6 and add that

much ash into the pit, measuring from the bottom. Ash will heap

up no more than two inches over the top of the pit.

A three-step visualization of the digging of a firepit.

Top: the undisturbed cave floor.

Middle: a fire pit is dug. Notice that the storage jar is

removed—it will never be seen again.

Bottom: we see the pit filled with ash and then, later, two

more strata added above.

subject: RPG / Solo Journaling RPGs / Thousand Year Old Campfire
text:
52 53

Find Entry 9 Storage Vessels

Pots sealed with wax, plant resin, or wooden wedges have been

intentionally left in the cave. The pots may be decorated with

abstract motifs. The containers often hold lost cultivars of seeds

that may still be viable. Add one or two seed pots to random

columns on the Strata Chart; they are each 2d6 inches high and will

already be half buried on a one or two out of six.

The seeds contained in the pots are most likely sunflower,

sumpweed, goosefoot, knotweed, or maygrass.

Find Entry 10 Potsherds

Potsherds have been left in the cave, present in 1d6 randomly

chosen columns. The pieces are numerous and small, almost

making a stratum of their own.

Find Entry 11 Human Remains

Human remains are found in the cave. This is most likely not a

full human body but disarticulated pieces. Decayed clothing and

possessions may accompany the remains.

Remains may be randomly placed in any column or columns.

Find Entry 12 Wall Markings

Petroglyphs, paintings, carvings, soot drawings, or other marks

have been made upon the walls. This category also includes later

vandalism using scratches, spray paint, or carbide lamp soot marks.

Early marks may be culturally significant, religious or mystical,

playful or just plain graffiti.

Prepare d6 markings for the wall. Each will be d6+4” in their longest

dimension (measuring by the ruler on the side of the Strata Chart). A

wall marking is placed in a random column about 3d6+30” above the

current soil level. Draw wall markings directly on the Strata Chart.

Use a dotted line or differently colored pencil to define what is a mark

on a wall. If the mark would extend off the top of the Strata Chart,

then it will appear in the Upper Cave Wall section.

Non-graffiti marks will not overlap other marks, though they may

combine with other prexisting marks. Graffiti marks will often

modify or overwrite non-graffiti marks.

Wall markings on the Strata Chart can and will become buried!

A note on rock art creation for this game:

Some rock art has spiritual or cultural meaning

for Native American tribes. Consider creating

new symbols rather than using historically

accurate shapes.

Find Entry 13 Recent Trash

Recent objects have been lost or abandoned in the cave. These are

usually objects that were not precious to the owners: flashlight

batteries, bits of outdoor gear, cigarette butts and bottlecaps, or food

and beverage containers.

Any given incident of recent trash placement may fill one to six

columns, as thematically appropriate.

subject: RPG / Solo Journaling RPGs / Thousand Year Old Campfire
text: 54 55 Find Entry 14 Prospecting A commercial prospector searched the cave for guano. In a random column on the Strata Chart dig a hole 2d6+3” deep. Spoil is heaped up next to the hole. If no guano is exposed, then the prospector leaves. If guano is exposed, extend the hole downwards to the bottom of the exposed guano layer and enlarge the width of the hole to approximately the width of a column. Add more spoil outside the hole. Damage or remove any items that are exposed by the prospector. On a 1-4 the guano prospector will refill the hole; otherwise it is left empty. It will mostly fill in during the placement of the next layer. Find Entry 15 Hidden Objects, Unburied An object made of cloth or leather has been placed in the cave in such a way that it is protected from damp, vermin, and casual detection. This might be a blanket, shirt, bag, or tool. The object was precious to the owner. It is decorated in some manner and may have ritual purpose. Draw the item along the top edge of the Strata Chart or in the Upper Cave Wall section. This object may be found by later cave visitors. Find Entry 16 Pothunters, Exploration Treasure hunters, early or amateur archeaologists, or other visitors have searched the cave for artifacts or treasure. They will search the cave, dig some holes, then leave things a mess. Any unburied items of Native American origin, hidden or not, are discovered and removed from the cave. Any visible non- Native-American objects are ignored unless obviously valuable. Visible human remains are immediately removed from the cave. The treasure hunters then dig exploratory holes. A first hole is dug in a random column that is 7d6” deep. An equivalent amount of spoil is heaped up, the heap centered on another column. If any Native American artifacts are uncovered then the hole will be widened until most of the artifacts in any grouping have been collected. If artifacts were discovered, or if rock art is visible on the walls, a second randomly placed hole will be dug that is 6d5” deep. The spoil from this hole will fill the first. This hole digging process will repeat as long as artifacts are found, but each hole will be one d6 shallower than the previous. Non-Native-American items displaced by the digging will be mixed into the spoil and ignored unless obviously valuable, in which case they will be removed from the cave. Uncovered human remains are removed from the cave. The spoil from each hole will be used to fill the previous holes as much as possible. The last hole will remain empty. The bottom of one randomly determined hole will receive a batch of new trash composed of food waste, bottles and cans, broken tools, and similar items. See Find Entry 13. All items removed by treasure hunters leave the cave and are never seen again. If no items are uncovered then the

subject: RPG / Solo Journaling RPGs / Thousand Year Old Campfire
text: bottom of one randomly determined hole will receive a batch of new trash composed of food waste, bottles and cans, broken tools, and similar items. See Find Entry 13. All items removed by treasure hunters leave the cave and are never seen again. If no items are uncovered then the treasure hunters leave disappointed.

subject: RPG / Solo Journaling RPGs / Thousand Year Old Campfire
text:
56 57

Find Entry 17 Homemaking

Conscientious residents have tidied up the cave, filling in holes,

disposing of unwanted material, and reducing mounds to floor level.

The longer term the residents, the more thorough the job.

Garbage is removed; garbage refers to anything visible on the cave floor

that isn’t valuable. Remains, potsherds, and broken tools are considered

garbage and dealt with as described below. Useful or valuable objects

will be kept and removed from the cave. Rocks line the firepit, and large

rocks or logs serve as furnishing.

If a hole is being filled, then the garbage will be placed into the bottom

of that hole. If there is no hole, then on a 1-3 the garbage will be piled in

the fire pit and burnable material destroyed; on a 4-6 trash will be taken

from the cave, disappearing from the archaeological record.

Find Entry 18 Erosion

Natural processes have eroded some of the cave floor. These processes

cross strata and disturb items. Erosion makes dips in the soil; there is

never sublimation or pooling of water.

To erode, determine a location, width, and depth for the erosion, then

determine the disposition of any affected items.

Randomly choose a column to be the center of the eroded area. Roll 4d6

to determine the depth of the eroded bowl in inches. Randomly choose

columns to the left and right of the eroded area; these are the edges of

the uneroded area.

Draw a gentle arc that moves from the uneroded area down to the

deepest point of the eroded area and then back up to the uneroded edge

on the other side. Erase all the strata within that arc.

Finds are affected in the following manner-

Wooden and cloth artifacts disappear or are destroyed

and are removed from the cave.

Bones remain, though complete skeletons are

disarticulated. Other items stay or go as seems appropriate.

Remaining material that is exposed is moved to the bottom

of the eroded bowl, but partially exposed items remain

where they are, sticking out of the strata.

Here we see soil displacement due to erosion. Dry

Prong Creek infrequently floods and water backs

up into Old Morris Cave, removing or adding soil.

subject: RPG / Solo Journaling RPGs / Thousand Year Old Campfire
text: 58 59 Find Entry 19 Animal Scavengers A scavenging animal has come rooting through the cave, searching out an easy meal. Scavengers will dig straight down for 3d6” toward the bone closest to the surface, making a hole about half the width of a column. Spoil from the digging is evenly spread around the cave and is not drawn on the Strata Chart. If the hole reaches the bone, the bone is removed from the cave, leaving an empty space. If the bone is not reached, the animal will abandon the attempt. If there is no bone in the cave, the animal will dig down toward the nearest item (but not toward a rock). The item will be taken from the hole and left on the surface in a new column. Find Entry 20 Furnishings A large rock or log has been brought into the cave for the convenience of its occupants. On a 1-3 the object is a log; on a 4-6 the object is a large rock. Rocks and logs will be approximately 3d6+5” in height and one column in width. They will be placed one column away from the fire pit. When it comes to items being moved around later in the history of the cave, large rocks and logs are a special case. They are never removed from the cave and are not used to line a fire pit, but a log may be burned in the firepit provided the entry would seem to indicate so. Anastomic Network: A network of channels formed by the dissolution of calcium carbonates through bedding planes in limestone. In the case of Old Morris Cave acidic groundwater created a porous, sponge-like path for positive-pressure cave outflow. Backfill: To restore a site by refilling an excavated hole with the unwanted material removed from it. d6: A six-sided die. 2d6 indicates two six-sided die. Figure x: The indicator for one of the flow charts found on pages 8-46 which describe events in the cave. A flow chart is operated with a d6. Icons on the flow charts indicate what finds will be added to the cave. Find: Any object or disturbance, natural or artificial, which is not a product of the processes that created or laid down the soil in that place. Find Entry: Instructions for modifying the cave strata and adding finds to the Strata Chart. The flow charts lead to Find Entries. Find Entries appear on pages 47-58. Gypsum: A soft mineral found in limestone beds. Sometimes used by Native Americans as medicine and in pottery. Mattock: A heavy tool used for prying, digging, and chopping. A standard item in an archaeologist’s toolkit and only used in as a last resort. Pincushioning: An anthropological term for a group of antagonists riddling a fallen enemy’s corpse with arrows (or engaging in other ritual violence) in order to share complicity in the death. Processual Archaeology: An outmoded school of archaeology that attempts to apply scientific method while interpreting the material record. Strata:

subject: RPG / Solo Journaling RPGs / Thousand Year Old Campfire
text: resort. Pincushioning: An anthropological term for a group of antagonists riddling a fallen enemy’s corpse with arrows (or engaging in other ritual violence) in order to share complicity in the death. Processual Archaeology: An outmoded school of archaeology that attempts to apply scientific method while interpreting the material record. Strata: Distinct layers of sedimentation. In the case of this book, strata are described by a dividing line and filled with a texture. Strata Chart: The chart which records the strata accumulated during game play. It is found on page seven. Upper Cave Wall Chart: An extension of the Strata Chart used only for wall art. It is found on page six. FURTHER NOTES ON THE DIG

subject: RPG / Solo Journaling RPGs / Thousand Year Old Campfire
text:
60 61

In Memoriam

Professor Timothy Brian Hutchings 1929-1979

The Old Morris Cave dig was to be Professor Hutchings’ last field

project before his early retirement in 1981. He had prepared for

the dig for years—arranging funding, coordinating with government

agencies, and successfully fighting a lawsuit brought by a local

Native American advocacy group.

This dig wasn’t just his project: in many ways it was Tim himself.

His ready wit and easy laughter filled the site with his enthusiasm.

He worked tirelessly alongside his graduate students and the

local volunteers, never hesitating to put his hand to even the most

grueling tasks. And throughout it all he was an educator, keeping

a running chatter about the procedural, historical, and pedagogical

aspects of the dig.

It was on the final evening of the dig that tragedy struck. Professor

Hutchings fell into Dry Prong Creek in the dark and drowned. The

creek waters were in semi-flood after a day of thunderstorms and

the waters ran fast, emptying into Green River. As of the time of

this publication, his body has not been recovered.

Professor Hutchings often joked he would “go out like the great

Heinrich Schliemann, who was found slumped over a partially

exposed stylobate with a beer bottle in one hand and a rock hammer

in the other... and who was later buried on a hillside overlooking the

site where he uncovered ancient Troy.” His wish was granted.

Professor Timothy Hutchings’ legacy lives on through the

accomplishments of the many students whose lives he touched

over his fifteen years in the university system. He will especially

be missed by us, his students on the Old Morris Cave dig team.

“I can only hope my students have

learned as much from me as

I have learned from them.”

Erica Chiles

Daphne Delagino

Yuriko Ebata

Sandra Maltz

Robert Mosley

Dawn Sherwood

Daniel Smith

Sarah Woolsey

subject: RPG / Solo Journaling RPGs / Thousand Year Old Campfire
text: To the Editor, Please consider including the following review of my late supervisor’s final study in your next issue of American Antiquity. Sincerely, Erica Chiles PhD Candidate Brandle University Department of Anthropology Old Morris Cave: A Continuous Use Campsite in Mammoth Cave National Park. TIMOTHY HUTCHINGS. St. Louis Museum of Ethnology & Brandle University Technical Series, No. 9, Peoria, IL, 1980. 1-60 pp., illus. $10.00. Reviewed by Erica Chiles, Brandle University Every once in a while an author succeeds in attracting attention for a work that merits critical acclaim. “Old Morris Cave” is not such a volume. It is a middling tome that conspicuously lacks theory given its pretentious claim to being a “processual archaeological survey,” which the author fails to define anywhere in the text. Indeed, there recently seems to be a growing distaste for abstract theory among archaeologists; a distaste that is clearly reflected here. Rather than defining terms or evaluating causal explanations, Hutchings was overly concerned with getting back to the earth and the chronology of this cave system. There is no concern for the cultur- al context of the cave’s inhabitants or the meanings that any of the archaeological finds may have held in these people’s minds. Hutchings’ theoretical approach is, in fact, ahistorical. Hutchings was deeply enamored of systems theory and he modeled human behavior using extremely rigid flow charts that presume no significant cultur al or technological developments took place at any time in the last 2,200 years. He has effectively reduced very real people into sim ple, procedural, predictable automatons with no imagination and very little agency. As such, the work is kind of a fantastic parody of applied processual archaeology. On the whole, the research of Hutchings and his ilk represent the interests of a predominately Western, white, male discourse. They seek to fashion an objective truth about the ‘real’ past that they can commodify, water down, and control for a passive, uneducated, entertainment-oriented public. Meanwhile they rob Native Americans of control over their own heritage and desecrate their ancestors’ graves and sacred spaces. It is long overdue, but archaeologists must now move beyond the nar- row, objectivist lens of processual archaeology. Archaeologists must recognize that any archaeological research that does not critique the dominant culture serves to lend it political support. Fieldwork, excavation, museum displays, and texts are not neutral instruments for approaching the past, and we must reject the idea that archae ology can remain politically neutral. We should instead turn to the rigorous study of power, negotiation, text, intertext, structure, ideology, and agency in both the past and in the present. Old Morris Cave” may serve some useful purpose as a historical document: It is an exemplar of a stagnant period of archaeological research. As the last (only?) work of a scholar of limited range, known more for his mistreatment of his graduate students than for his contributions to the field, this volume will always remind me that even the worst of us will eventually perish. *** A related

subject: RPG / Solo Journaling RPGs / Thousand Year Old Campfire
text: stagnant period of archaeological research. As the last (only?) work of a scholar of limited range, known more for his mistreatment of his graduate students than for his contributions to the field, this volume will always remind me that even the worst of us will eventually perish. *** A related news item: As the second official act of his administra- tion Kentucky Governor John Y. Brown has declared Old Morris Cave and its surroundings a heritage park. There will be no more digs in the area of the cave without permission and oversight from represen- tatives of regional Cherokee/Chickasaw/Shawnee tribal groups. There will be a formal ceremony and barring of the cave on Sept first of this year.

1 2 3 4 5 6

Methodology Summary

1. Roll a d6 and Count up that many inches from the current

surface line. STart at “begin” on the first Roll.

2. draw a roughly horizontal line to indicate the new top layer.

allow the line to gently undulate and react to previous finds.

3. consult ANY FIGURE NUMBER indicated, and then navigate the

associated flow chart using a d6.

4. AS DIRECTED BY THE FLOW CHART, MAKE CHANGES TO AND ADD OBJECTS

ATOP THE NEW STRATUM, AND ADD OR CHANGE MARKS ON THE “UPPER

CAVE WALL.”

5. Fill the new stratum in with a unique pattern to differentiate it

from other layers. Samples appear on the left side of the chart.

6. Return to line one and repeat.
